rules.when: The task depends on fresh news, current events, live public facts, or recent market and product changes.
rules.why: Grok is the preferred dispatch when current web-connected context is central to the work.

rules.when: The task is a trivial mechanical edit such as a rote rename, formatting sweep, targeted typo fix, or simple file gathering.
rules.why: Use the cheapest fast profile when the task is narrow and low ambiguity.

rules.when: The task is a big or ambiguous multi-file feature, a risky refactor, or work that requires holding many moving parts in mind.
rules.why: Use a strong coding profile for broad design and implementation work.

rules.when: The task changes this firstmate repo's own supervision backbone: bin/fm-watch.sh, bin/fm-guard.sh, a harness turnend-guard hook, or other logic the always-on watcher/guard rely on for correctness.
rules.why: A narrow set of scripts backs every other task's supervision; a subtle regression here is silent and fleet-wide, so it earns deeper reasoning than the general firstmate-repo default even though the model tier does not need to change.

rules.when: Safety-critical, security-sensitive, or hard-to-reverse work: schema or data migrations, auth/session/credential changes, payment or billing logic, or anything touching a frozen or safety-critical spec.
rules.why: A model reviewing its own irreversible change has anchoring bias; an independent pass and the strongest available model are both cheap relative to the cost of a bad migration or a broken safety-flow.

rules.when: A genuine architectural or product tradeoff with long-term consequences that the crewmate must reason through and justify, not just implement.
rules.why: The deliverable's entire value is the quality of the argued judgment call; this is low-volume, high-stakes work, so the cost delta of the top model is bounded and well spent.
